evidence_quotes.quote: Mandatory notification and transparency when publishers plan to delist or disable a game.
Requirements to maintain basic access to single-player or offline content after delisting, especially for previously sold titles.
Regulations preventing server dependencies from breaking games that customers legally own, unless an alternative or patch is provided.
Support for archival and preservation efforts, potentially in collaboration with libraries, museums, or public institutions.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, Thank you for the opportunity to contribute to the consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act. This initiative is a vital step toward ensuring fairness, transparency, and accountability in todays rapidly evolving digital marketplace. I would like to respectfully raise an important and growing concern regarding the preservation of digital video games and the rights of consumers who purchase them. In recent years, hundreds of games have already been permanently removed or made inaccessible, often even to those who legally purchased them. In some cases, even physical copies are rendered useless due to online-only access requirements or server dependencies. This practice raises serious concerns about consumer rights, digital ownership, and long-term cultural preservation. Customers invest in these products expecting ongoing access, especially in cases where no clear end-of-service timelines are communicated at the point of sale. Therefore, I urge the Commission to consider stronger protections for consumers in the Digital Fairness Act, particularly by introducing provisions such as: Mandatory notification and transparency when publishers plan to delist or disable a game. Requirements to maintain basic access to single-player or offline content after delisting, especially for previously sold titles. Support for archival and preservation efforts, potentially in collaboration with libraries, museums, or public institutions. Regulations preventing server dependencies from breaking games that customers legally own, unless an alternative or patch is provided. This issue is not just a niche concern. The Stop Killing Games initiative has gathered over one million signatures across the EU, signaling broad public support for legislative action to protect digital ownership and access to creative works. Video games are not only entertainment products but also a form of modern culture, storytelling, and artistic expression. Without action, we risk losing a vital part of our digital heritageand the trust of millions of consumers who simply wish to access the products they purchased in good faith. Thank you for your consideration. I welcome and support the Commissions continued leadership in shaping a fairer digital future for all Europeans.
